mainEntity.name: How much does an OM654/OM656 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM654/OM656 typically costs £4,500–£7,500 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by cylinder count and power output. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on labour rates. A replacement motor for the OM654 averages around £5,374 for a good used unit, with new/reconditioned examples reaching £10,624.

mainEntity.name: Is the OM654/OM656 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654/OM656 are generally considered reliable, earning 3.5★ out of 5 [ESTIMATED] — some owners have passed 150,000–200,000 miles without problems, but valvetrain wear (camshafts and rockers) is reported around 100,000 miles. Later models benefited from improved internal parts.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common OM654/OM656 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the OM656, vacuum pump failure is a critical issue — the combined oil/vacuum pump can develop an internal seal leak, allowing oil into the vacuum system and potentially causing loss of braking assistance. This typically occurs between 25,000–57,000 miles. Camshaft and rocker wear is another reported issue across both engines.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes models use the OM654/OM656?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 (2.0L I4) powers the C‑Class, E‑Class, GLC, A‑Class, B‑Class, CLA, GLA, GLB, Vito, and Sprinter. The OM656 (3.0L I6) powers the S‑Class, GLE, GLS, G‑Class, and higher-output E‑Class and CLS models.

mainEntity.name: How long does the OM654/OM656 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With meticulous maintenance and frequent oil changes, 150,000–200,000 miles is achievable, and some taxi‑use examples have exceeded 350,000 kilometres (≈217,000 miles). However, high‑mileage examples often require significant expenditure on valvetrain or vacuum pump work.

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz OM654/OM656 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The OM654 four-cylinder and OM656 straight-six are Mercedes' newest modular diesel engines — efficient and powerful, but valvetrain wear and vacuum pump failures are documented weaknesses. Backed by real UK enquiries [ESTIMATED].

rows.value: 15+ (A, B, C, E, S, CLS, GLA, GLC, GLE, GLS, G, Vito, Sprinter)

verdictRating.confidence: 85% confidence — based on owner forums, specialist reports, and documented TSBs; proprietary enquiry figures are estimated.

rows.score: 8 (Valvetrain wear and vacuum pump failure often require engine-out work; complex modern diesel)
12 (Available but some components (camshafts, vacuum pumps) are specialist-order items)
TBC (Depends on host vehicle value — see R-to-V flag guidance)
TBC/100 (Sum of the five rows above)

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: The OM654 and OM656 are refined, efficient modern diesels — but camshaft/rocker wear and vacuum pump failures are expensive to fix — buy only with full service history and extended warranty cover.
verdictRating.bestFor: High-mileage drivers who value refinement and efficiency and are prepared for specialist maintenance costs.
verdictRating.avoidIf: You need bulletproof, low-maintenance reliability on a tight budget — the older OM651 is simpler and more proven.

compatibility.crossBrandNote: Cross-link: Exclusive to Mercedes-Benz — no cross-brand fitment.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an OM654/OM656 typically takes 10–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED]. Vacuum pump replacement is an engine-out job on some variants.
costGuide.sharedCostNote: Exclusive to Mercedes-Benz — no cross-brand cost comparison applies.
